classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

title: Measure in Some Sense is Suitable to God Himself.

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

verses.book: Concerning the Nature of Good, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: But God cannot be said to have measure, lest He should seem to be spoken of as limited.

verses.book: Concerning the Nature of Good, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Yet He is not immoderate by whom measure is bestowed upon all things, so that they may in any measure exist.

verses.book: Concerning the Nature of Good, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Nor again ought God to be called measured, as if He received measure from any one.

verses.book: Concerning the Nature of Good, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: But if we say that He is the highest measure, by chance we say something; if indeed in speaking of the highest measure we mean the highest good.

verses.book: Concerning the Nature of Good, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: For every measure in so far as it is a measure is good; whence nothing can be called measured, modest, modified, without praise, although in another sense we use measure for limit, and speak of no measure where there is no limit, which is sometimes said with praise as when it is said: "And of His kingdom there shall be no limit."

verses.book: Concerning the Nature of Good, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: For it might also be said, "There shall be no measure," so that measure might be used in the sense of limit; for He who reigns in no measure, assuredly does not reign at all.
